sentences.text: --tape number three.
We-- when we just left we were talking about pushing the German units back--
Pushing, yeah, when the--
After Stalingrad, there was no-- they couldn't keep it up.
What-- the American was coming on one side, you know?
And the Russian on the other side.
They were pushing them.
They were throwing everything away.
They brought you on.
They change a lot.
They-- but there was no cooking now, for extra, for the officers.
That's what--
How far did we go?
I told you they were pushing, pushing.
How far we was going?
We were coming almost with Schachendorf, Austria.
No, no, you went through [INAUDIBLE] again.
You understand?
They're pushing, pushing, pushing.
End of it, we-- we-- we stayed in-- in Schachendorf.
Were you in Munkacs along the way?
Yes, I was in Munkacs over there too.
Yeah, brief stop.
Two days, three days only.
In Munkacs-- excuse me.
In Munkacs we were then-- when they said the war is over, that the Hungarians put down the guns, they don't want to fight no more against the Russians.
Right there.
Munkacs was the only city, I think, in the whole world, was 50% Jews.
But they-- they were [INAUDIBLE] Jews, you know?
And from there on, they were coming there.
Then I went there for that.
I could go to the city and get a-- a few things, what I wanted.
They were pushing us back, pushing us back with Russian again.
You know?
Then after Russians, they say, there's no stop.
There's no reason to stay here.
Again, we went back to Schachendorf.
That was right on the-- that was-- a time [INAUDIBLE].
But that day we got liberated.
That day.
But I can't believe it. 12 o'clock, midnight, it comes to the store.
The war is over.
And everything is gone.
And I had two cousins there who was in the white band.
They were-- they were half Jews or Catholic or their mother, father.
But they-- those boys said-- they said, everybody can go how they want to.
But I says-- I went over to there.
I said, Stevie, George, come on.
In a half hour, you free.
We are-- we were hearding from-- from Schachendorf how long they were-- that was right next to [INAUDIBLE], where we went out first, by the border.
We were holding all the guns and everything.
I says, commander, what are you going for, there?
Oh, they not going to hurt us no more.
So what did they do?
There was about 120 of these white band.
Those son-of-a-bitches Hungarians-- Hungarian Germans, they were.
They put them in a barn.
Now, this is the truth.
This is true worst thing I ever heard in my life.
They put them in a barn.
They threw around, gasoline.
And while they were running around, they shot every 120 of them.
This is the truth.
After liberation.
My cousins' two kids.
[INAUDIBLE] boys.
Where I told you, beautiful, big, tall guy-- they said, [INAUDIBLE], they're not going to hurt us.
We're the [INAUDIBLE].
We always-- we're-- we got a white band.
They were maybe eight years old when they convert.
That was the worst.
Then we work--
I lost everything.
I-- I said, there is no God.
How did-- how could they kill those little kids there, 2 to 10 years old?
How could they kill them?
What did they do?
Did they hurt you? you? you?
They didn't hurt nobody.
They're born in the right-- wrong place at the wrong time.
When I come back, I went right away to-- after the war, I went there, where they lived.
And they asked me, did-- did-- did I see the boys?
[INAUDIBLE] was one of them.
They were brought up Catholic, these two boys.
You know?
And I told them what happened.
They got a big memorial put up for the two boys, for [INAUDIBLE], when I go to the Seder during my aunt's, they buried.
She was 83 years old.
They convert.
And that's about 12 miles from [INAUDIBLE], where we used to live in-- in the-- in the winter.
I showed you.
And they-- my aunts and my-- they have here, [INAUDIBLE] And they take-- those-- those old people take care of theirs, where they buried.
And for the two boys, they put up a big monument in [INAUDIBLE].
They loved those two boys.
Some Hungarians was worse what the Germans.
I told you, when you want for water-- we want a drink of water.
Should I tell you about my cousin?
My cousin that-- that somebody tell your husband what he done with his wife?
You know, I don't know you girls know it.
But in Europe, that time was that the younger cannot get-- the younger cannot get married so long the older is not married.
My aunt had three daughters, Olga, [INAUDIBLE] and [?
Terry. ?] Terry was the youngest.
Olga was the oldest.
And [INAUDIBLE] was the middle one.
In 1919, my father brought up Olga for two weeks to see Chicago.
[INAUDIBLE] fall in love there with a schoolteacher.
His was [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and but she swore for her mother, how long she's alive, she's never going to marry Steve.
For he's a [INAUDIBLE].
OK?
20 Years, she was her girlfriend.
She lived there in the hotel.
Every morning they brought him up the breakfast.
Then he went to school.
Night, his supper.
He was the big boss.
They had a three-story building, a hotel.
The wealthiest people.
There were three girls and one boy.
All right, the-- the-- the two younger-- the younger one and the older they took away.
And the brother, Joe, they took him away.
And the parents died but 10 years before that.
So he tells his wife, [INAUDIBLE], don't worry.
I'll hide you.
You won't have no problem.
He hide her for awhile, for two, three weeks.
That minute when the Germans comes in, that son-of-a-- I don't want to call him-- was the first to tell where his wife is hiding.
He never thought the Jewish people are going to come back from this concentration camp.
There was two boys with us whose name were [INAUDIBLE], from the capital, [INAUDIBLE].
One of them, [INAUDIBLE], was the European champion in that.
So he used to bring up, in the black market, veal and this, and what you couldn't sell at that time.
He says, you know what your brother-in-law done?
I says, no.
He said, he was the first one to tell where his wife was hiding.
We took care of him.
What do you mean, you took care of him?
We took care of that son-of-a-bitch.
We killed him.
We kicked him to death.
Four of us, five of us.
He didn't deserve more.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I know I was wrong.
How would you like-- you see, what I told you I was in a very exclusive store in Budapest, every morning we had-- we had to put-- you know, those heavy-- you know, the white thing on.
Moniker-- moniker every day.
I went to a barber there for 8 or 10 years.
He had there a barber.
He he was a hunchback.
And every-- every Sunday, he come up to shave me.
Well, I give them a good tip.
And that minute, when Hitler comes in, I go in to take a shave.
You goddamn I-don't-know-what-kind-of-Jew.
Get outta here or I'll cut your neck.
How would you feel?
The guy was going, thank you, thank you.
I-- it's all right.
Eight years, he always got a good tip.
Eight years.
Every Sunday morning, come to shave me.
With a good tip, everything.
At that minute, Hitler comes and he was the-- he was the first to say, get out of this chair.
I'll cut your neck.
There was a lot of retaliation, in every city in the world.
Look what was in Italy, right?
In Budapest, yes.
I-- I didn't see this.
But I come back later on.
You understand?
This-- I come much later.
And Budapest was liberated much before.
There were-- wait a minute.
You must know where it was.
There-- how you come out from the-- the [INAUDIBLE], where that girl was there.
There was-- every day they put up there-- they-- they hanged up a couple of these Nazis there.
I never saw that.
I told you, I never saw these things.
What, I never did.
When I come back to Budapest, it was normal life then, by that time.
No, no, no.
We didn't want no revenge.
We just wanted to come home.
And that day I was liberated, today is 52 years.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I never thought it.
Especially when I was in Lemberg, in a death role.
Nobody in the world.
Except my niece, in Israel.
Not my niece, my cousin's daughter.
Friends, yes.
Yeah, they were friends who they-- who they-- they were in the Swedish home.
Just only my aunt.
Nobody.
Everybody got killed.
I put ad in all kinds of paper.
I looking for persons.
When I was in Washington DC, with the Holocaust, when Reagan was there, all kinds of ads, asking for Reichfeld or [INAUDIBLE].
I didn't find nobody, anyone.
But the mistake, when I was in Budapest, I told my cousin's daughters in Israel, I didn't even know her name.
I told you that.
I didn't even know her name.
When-- when I was there they said that [INAUDIBLE], well, I saw your sister, brother, right away in the gas, what were too little.
She's in Israel.
I says, where?
She says, we don't know what's the name.
They said, we don't know where they took off for different kinds of name.
I said, who knows that?
[INAUDIBLE], when I got back from Budapest, I called her right away up.
She says, somebody in Haifa knows that-- where she is and what's her name.
I says, give me the lady's number.
I know the name is-- lady's number was Smith.
Her husband was a lawyer at that time.
So she says to me, call me in two weeks.
I called her in two weeks.
She says, [?
George, ?] I still don't know where she lives.
I says, give me this lady's name.
Excuse me.
She give me his-- this-- this lawyer's name.
I call her up.
I says, I heard you know where [INAUDIBLE] lives in Israel.
She says, who are you?
I say, she's my cousin's daughter.
I'm [?
George Reichfeld. ?] I don't know you, I says.
I know that I says, I don't think you know me.
I says, could you tell me what's her name and where she lives?
She says to me, if you call me back a week, I'll let you know.
I'll-- I know-- I don't-- she lives in the kibbutz, I showed you, right on the Lebanon border, where the Jordan River is.
You know?
Well, I got pictures from there.
And I called her a week.
She says look, I still don't know.
I says, you know what?
Next week I'm going to be in-- in Tel Aviv.
I told my girlfriend, come on, Jean, let's go to Israel.
So we went to Tel Aviv.
I called this lady up.
She started to talk to me, Hebrew.
I don't even understand Jewish, not Hebrew.
So I went down to the desk.
There was a lot of Romanian there-- Romanian Jews.
They all took Hebrew.
And-- and she write up 10 Hebrew name in the kibbutz.
The first seven, I only know two names-- Susan, a Hungarian.
Find Susan, a Hungarian in Israel.
The first seven says they never heard, they never heard, they never heard.
Then I come out.
Right as you come out from the Hilton, there's a little police station.
I says, you speak English?
[EXHALES]
He says, a little.
I says, will you please call this dame up?
Susan, a Hungarian.
But-- this kibbutz, there was just three kibbutz left over.
He says, no.
There was two left over.
No.
He calls the third one.
He says, she don't live no more in the kibbutz.
She lives down in the village.
I don't want to-- [CRYING]
I says, will you call her, please?
She called her up.
He called her up.
I said, [INAUDIBLE].
She was surprised.
Nobody know her [INAUDIBLE].
I said, [INAUDIBLE].
They [INAUDIBLE].
But you are you.
[INAUDIBLE], I says, this is [INAUDIBLE].
She must fall down and fainted.
Two minutes later, her husband picks her up the phone.
Who are you?
Who are you?
And then [INAUDIBLE] come to the phone.
I don't want to tell you how we feeled.
She says, right away, the Lebanon the border, I showed you where, where the Jordan River is, I says, [INAUDIBLE], I'm coming out tomorrow.
She says, [?
George, ?] you'll never find that.
So next day morning, she says, George-- I says, when you coming?
She says, we get up a little later.
But I'll be there, 11 o'clock.
I told my girlfriend, here's some money.
Go out shopping.
When-- when she comed in there, in the Hilton, you know, I don't want to tell you what a relief was that.
Only one left over.
I got there on the cape-- whenever I did-- 10 years, 11 years ago now. 12 years ago.
She was twice in-- I-- I bring them up to Boston.
They were twice here.
And now she-- she goes to Budapest to fix up her teeth as one for [INAUDIBLE].
So I-- I-- I take a place for them to the-- in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE], and the market island.
You know?
That's the life story.
Nobody left out--
Yeah.
Yeah, 12-- 12 years exactly now.
OK.
I want to go back to Budapest following the war.
Yeah.
After the war, I-- well, should I be honest?
I'm honest.
First, honest, I got first a job.
The farmers used to bring in gooses, chicken eggs.
And I used to help them take it off from the truck.
I got a pretty nice pay.
One day, a lady brings-- at that time you couldn't bring in bacon or this or that.
And she says, I got here 10 pounds of bacon. 10 kilo bacon.
Can you sell it?
And then the little store start open.
There was a butcher named Schwartz.
And I know the guy a long time ago.
I go to him.
I says, [INAUDIBLE], can you use 10 kilo bacon?
So yeah, he says, sure.
How much you give it to-- how much you pay me?
He said, how much you got?
I says, about 10 kilo.
He said, I'll give you 80 cents for a kilo.
And I went back.
I said, I just got 60 cents.
That's good.
So I made $2 on it.
She said, but if you want I can bring you a whole hog in.
A whole hog in.
Brought in the hog.
I made 50 bucks on it.
Then I heard that they bring bacon and eggs.
You get-- you can get a permit to bring for the English, American, French, and the Russian embassy, all these chickens, eggs, no bacon and no fat.
No nothing of these kinds of things.
So I got in touch there with a Russian, who know how to do it.
So what did we do?
We went out in the farm.
In the farm we bought salt.
And we went to Romania to buy salt for two cents a kilo that time.
Went to the farmers.
We give them four-- four kilo salt for a kilo bacon.
And for a kilo bacon, we got a gram gold.
So our money got 40 times.
We doubled our money in a day.
In two days.
So end of it, business was so-- but easy.
[INAUDIBLE] told me, forget it.
I was the biggest.
I had over four trucks going every two weeks up to Vienna.
The bottom was the bacon, the lard, the butter.
On the top was eggs, chicken, cheese, turkey.
And I had a thing.
They paid everything in dollar.
So I had the right to have dollars on me.
But I never had a-- you know how it is.
That's-- that what was to be my last trip.
That was not legal.
You asked me--
That was black market.
You ask me a question.
I'll tell you.
Everybody done it.
Everybody done it.
Oh, there was organized-- there was every-- don't forget in Vienna, at that time, you couldn't get nothing.
And in there, in all of those countries, in Vienna, at that time, was over 100 Hungarian Christians who was with coal and diamonds and everything.
They the one who made the whole business.
End of it, I didn't want to do it.
[INAUDIBLE]-- [INAUDIBLE] says, [?
George, ?] you got enough.
You got enough.
But you know, the money, it was coming in.
If we wanted to buy a bag of sugar, you needed a bag-- there-- there, you know, there was no money. 100 blue money, 50 gray money, this in color money.
Even-- you didn't even know what you payed for something.
The money was coming in.
You wouldn't believe it.
People got millionaire.
That's the reason you see so many big Hungarian guys is all full of money.
They're multimillionaire.
They didn't have time to stop the black market.
And I was cut.
At that time, the captain was named Dr.
[INAUDIBLE].
He says to me, [?
George, ?] get out of this business.
I had, at that time, 100 Napoleon.
That's a gold 20 franc.
It's a rooster on it.
They took everything away.
And I-- I run away with a suit and a toothbrush.
I never looked back.
When was that?
In 19-- I tell you I had to pay for-- that must be 1946, '47.
I went to Vienna first, to the Rothschild's.
Then we went in Germany, different camps.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
I was in Ulm, where Einstein was born.
I was there.
There it come out aloud that those people who was born in Chicago-- was born in United States can go, right away, back.
So I went up to Munich.
And I says, I was born in Chicago, this and this.
My father died there.
We were American citizen in 1921.
I used to live on Cedric Street, North Hudson Street.
I went to school.
Everything was shine.
Two weeks later, they called me in.
Get packed and come into Bremerhaven.
But I didn't tell them I give up my citizenship.
I went back.
Just before, two hours, the ship would start from Bremerhaven, they took me off the ship.
He says, you're a trader.
You give up your citizenship.
Back.
And I stayed [INAUDIBLE].
And then I come back in 1950-- '52.
I was staying in Germany.
I was in Ulm, [INAUDIBLE].
I was-- I was working there too.
I was playing soccer there.
And I was working-- working in the warehouse.
I had two jobs there.
And the end of it, in 1952, they-- all of camps were [INAUDIBLE] then.
I come back to United States.
My first job was here, I was a chicken flicker.
And a broad [INAUDIBLE] in a street corner.
Now, I want to tell you something you don't believe.
My friend Larry, there, I would-- it's funny thing.
We-- the same boat we went back to Hungary, that time, the George Washington, we were coming back with the same boat.
But 50 people there was waiting for me in the-- on the-- in the what's the name?
On the piers, in-- you know, in Long Island, where the boat comes in.
And my friends, they're waiting for me there.
I says, how did you know?
He says, I read it in the paper that George Reichfeld was born in Chicago and went back to Hungary and went through everything, is coming back to the United States.
And he was waiting for me.
And how did you know him?
From where?
From Germany.
We lived together.
Actually, he's a wonderful guy.
I'll show you a picture of him.
First I was a chicken flicker.
I was a cake-- I learned to be a cake baker in Brookline, by Feldman's Bakery, next to the KI.
Bad marriage.
I told you-- I told you again, I was married.
I was married.
Just with-- with [?
Dustin ?] [?
Hoffman, ?] she left me there with two kids.
I never saw her.
Now it's over 40 years.
I didn't even see her.
I don't know where the hell she is.
And I asked the kids, who wants to-- if you get married, you want to see the kids-- your mother?
They said, what kind of mother?
Did she ever say-- see us?
I don't even know where she is.
I heard that she had three more kids.
That's my life story.
One is-- one is Steven.
One is Benji.
They were-- they were-- they're now 40 years old.
And 42.
One-- she-- she had Benji before I married her.
She had a-- a-- the father was an Irish guy.
Her-- my ex-wife's father was a Greek Jew.
The mother was a Christian.
[INAUDIBLE].
Should I be honest with you?
Some way, God was good to me.
And they always say, a working man will make a living, never going to be rich.
Right?
I had some money coming back from Hungary.
And my friend, Larry, there, we first started a little smutty business, making woman panties.
And then from panties we went to sportswear, this and that.
I had a marvelous life in United States.
It could not be better.
I traveled three times, all over the world.
Yesterday night they were talking about Bali.
And I said, what a wonderful time I had there in Bali too.
And I just looked at-- there in-- then when I went twice to Alaska, I loved it.
I went to the World's Fair.
I come to this country.
I couldn't-- nothing.
And my first date, I had a date by [INAUDIBLE].
I don't think-- at that time [INAUDIBLE] was, on Beacon Street, a very good restaurant.
And then my first date-- my first-- on Saturday, I was the happiest guy, when I first bought a-- a blue suit by Ripley's.
I was looking that everybody's looking at me, that I got a suit.
I have no complaints.
And never thought I'd get sick. 42 years ago I had a heart attack. 40 years, I didn't even go to a doctor.
I never took a pill in.
I traveled a lot.
The whole summer, every year, I stayed on the Cape in Centerville, a two-bedroom apartment.
In January, I always go to Acapulco for two weeks.
In May, to Budapest, Hungary.
Summer on the Cape.
And fall, I go away again.
So I can say, right, God was good to me.
I have no complaints.
But now, listen here, all of-- see, what I told you, 40 years later, 42, for 42 years ago I had a heart attack. 40 years, I didn't even go to a doctor.
OK.
I got angina.
I got a pacemaker.
I got a dislocated disc.
And I take 14 pills in a day.
And I still date.
I still go out.
I'm not going to feel sorry for myself.
I-- if I would feel sorry for myself, I don't think I could live that long.
And I better have 100 friends to 1 enemy.
I used to live in Norwood, in the [?
Windsor ?] [?
Garden. ?] Nobody-- nobody know my name, except the Cookie Man.
Where every day I brought the kids a big box of chocolate chip cookies or cupcakes, or half and half.
I used to take them to the children's zoo.
In the winter I-- I rented out the snowmobiles.
I used to take the kids around.
I had a pretty-- I tell you why, because I told you.
I've never smoked.
I never drinked.
The only thing, I gambled when I got married.
No, I used to gamble.
I used to go up to Foxborough, with my date, give her $20 to play.
But I'm not a gambler.
I love the theater.
And I love traveling.
And I like nice restaurants.
And nice ladies.
Number one, be good to yourself.
How long you don't hurt nobody, it's nobody's goddamn business what you do.
And the most important thing in your life, try to meet the right people.
Try always to look a little higher, where you are.
I can show you my last week lady friends was all doctors and this.
And I couldn't-- my first one was in Worcester, the wealthiest woman there.
She says, George, you're not educated.
But you're enough intelligence.
And what I told you, try to-- try to look a little higher where you are.
Well, I told you I got no schooling.
I told you that.
And I'm very careful who I go out with, where I live.
I love nice restaurants.
And I love the theater.
And I love-- traveling has gone by me.
I don't think I can travel again.
You imagine, go down to Florida, go down to Budapest, go down to Israel in nine months.
Well, what I told you-- number one, be good to yourself.
That's the most important thing in the world.
You think I'm right or wrong?
If you're no good to yourself, you're no good to nobody.
That's the way I feel.
Maybe I'm wrong.
You're very welcome.
On on the left-hand side is my father.
His name was-- [INAUDIBLE] or Riechfeld.
Right next to him there is a-- name is [INAUDIBLE].
He's a Hungarian.
He was a good friend of my father.
Next is [INAUDIBLE].
He's a friend of my father.
And this guy who's coming up, his name is [INAUDIBLE], Frank [INAUDIBLE].
His-- his son was my best friend.
After that, I don't know what happened.
This-- this guy's a Polish guy, I don't know.
And this name is [INAUDIBLE].
He's a German.
This was taken in Chicago, 1920, on Sedgwick Street, by Oscar Mayer.
Remember the factory down there on Sedgwick Street?
No.
Way down.
Mm.
I was young.
[LAUGHS] Go ahead.
The left-hand side is Benji.
He was, there, about a year and four months old, I think.
Today he's 42.
With him is Steven.
He's a year and two months younger what he is.
This was taken in 1955.
In-- in the house.
In Boston, Brookline.
On Harvard Street, 406 Harvard--
406 Harvard Street.
This is my cousin, only live relative I got left over.
The whole family got killed.
Her name is Susan.
What's her Jewish name?
I don't even know.
I don't even know her last Jewish name.
Her name is [INAUDIBLE].
It was originally [INAUDIBLE], but it come from Hungary, from [INAUDIBLE].
This was taken 10 years ago, exactly.
That was in Israel, right on Lebanon-- oh, right on the Lebanon border, in Israel.
This is my son, Steven.
This was taken on the Lebanon Jordan River, but 10 years ago too.
And that's me.
He's a doctor.
[LAUGHS]
